item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm getting Bacterial Contamination vibes from this.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip would make a fine addition to SCP: Containment Breach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahhhh! I get it! Thank yah!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It isn't actually hostile though, and that's kind of the worst part.
Look at what it says in addendum 1:

SCP-2878 [in Spanish again]: Complete. Friend. Metal. Disgust.

It wants to be a real boy so it can be friends with the meat-robots. Everything it does is an attempt to achieve that goal… the problem being that it is a completely naive and alien mind with no concept of things like "using people as spare parts and eating the leftovers is not conducive to friendship". It flat out doesn't comprehend that anything it has done might be construed as hostile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pseudo-interview log in Addendum 1 is absolutely chilling. It certainly gives off the impression that the victim is still "alive" in the sense that -2878 is acting as a life support system for the nervous system (which I assumed included the brain, but I could be mistaken) and the victim is screaming for help, not realizing it's dead already. Horrifying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't one of the tags say hostile?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But then again, I have a terribly unhealthy fondness for nonhuman psychology.

You and me both. This is one of three drafts I've had sloshing around my harddrive for years and they're all some variation on "what is this thing even thinking? is this thing even thinking?"

I like the angle of its sapience (even sentience) being questionable, which you can hopefully get a glimpse of in the article itself. If I end up doing some test logs or other additional materials I'll definitely explore that, but for now I'm just gonna iron out the last few technical flaws and get on with the next thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Slaving in computer terms is a specific type of connection in which one piece of hardware is under the control of another piece of hardware. Slaving one computer to another, for instance, allows the "master" computer to access the files on the "slave"'s harddrives, use its cpu for additional processing power, access peripherals attached to that computer, etc- but the slave cannot control the master computer in the same manner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nerve tissue slaved onto microprocessors throughout body.

I think I'm dumb but I don't know what "slaved" here means. I looked up "to slave" and didn't find anything that made sense.

—-

I overall feel like it didn't go far enough. My interest was semi-piqued at the end but, yaknow, that's the end. I could see it going into an interesting place in which people are made to think this is a sapient entity or something but it was merely coincidence and pre-programmed behavior. But then again, I have a terribly unhealthy fondness for nonhuman psychology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one's not doing it for me. The twist at the end was very predictable, and without that it's just a weird monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll definitely have a look at some of those wordings. As noted the surgical steel thing is an actual classification system and the implication is that it's built out of surgical steel and stuff from radio shack, but it's not like "technically correct but confusing" is a good place to be.

As for your "how do they know this" comments, I think we can safely assume that the SCP document is a summary of tests and observations performed in the past and that we don't necessarily have to explain how every piece of information was acquired. I mean, how do we know that SCP-1193 is "genetically normal"? Presumably they did a DNA test. How do we know that "The nervous system of members of SCP-962-1 is slaved to implanted electrodes"? Presumably they caught and dissected one at some point. How do we know that SCP-860 "can fit in any door lock that requires a key located in the area of the given coordinates"? Presumably they tried it on every lock they could find.

So how do we know that SCP-2878 used to be covered in sheet steel? Well for starters it's still got bits and pieces, and presumably there's some record of what it used to look like, maybe because the damage was sustained during containment. How do we know it's smart? Presumably it demonstrated problem-solving abilities during testing or while observed in the field. How do we know that it kills people and steals their organs? Presumably they shoved a D-class into its enclosure to see what would happen. Etc., etc.

+ More specific points:
- More specific points:

I find it difficult to imagine that the Foundation can ascribe psychology to something that they've only just started to communicate with. I think this would be better describing behaviour, and letting readers judge psychology for themselves.

As per above I disagree that Foundation wouldn't know anything about the way it behaves, but you have a point about show don't tell. I might try to think up something a bit more descriptive, but I'm also considering writing some testing logs to show off the weirdness.

Attacking on sight is not predatory behaviour.

I know. That's why it says "and extremely hostile". In that context I feel 'predatory' is an appropriate description for something that chases down, kills, eats and does unspeakable things to other creatures. Do you disagree?

Addendum 1 says the relevant incident was the first time that the skip had attempted to integrate new parts. As a result, I don't think the Foundation can be so certain that this is what the skip always does to its victims.

The operative word here is "new". That paragraph clearly states that the SCP normally replaces parts; this is the first instance of adding parts.

And if this thing has vocal cords, but no mouth, tongue or lips, how could it possibly create sounds that are intelligible as words.

Handwaving, mostly. This thing can clearly do really weird things with both mechanical and biological parts, presumably it modified those parts in some way.

Also note that the interview log does imply that it is difficult to understand. The log makes the conversation seem clearer than it would have been at the time because it's a transcript compiled from a recording after the fact. There's actually no indication that Dr. G understands anything it's saying except for his name and the word "help".

How is this thing expected to know it's name?

It's policy, and therefore habit, to refer to sapient SCPs by their designation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, well that's just my ignorance then. Can't always cater to ignorance of your audience :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll mention that 316 surgical steel is a grade of stainless steel that is commonly used in implants.

Although, yes, it feels a bit ambiguous in writing. Leaving the "316" off might make it clearer overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is the core of an interesting idea here, but I'm not sold on the presentation at present. It could have done with a lot more polishing before being posted. Leaving aside the final note (which, now that the Keter request is obfuscated, doesn't seem to have a reason to exist) the Series I style is not particularly logically cogent, and leaves me with a number of questions:

+ Expand questions
- Collapse questions

recontainment becomes unfeasible

While "unfeasible" is technically correct, I would suggest that "infeasible" is more common.

vaguely humanoid entity

The imprecision of "vaguely humanoid" (in what way is it not humanoid?) conflicts with clinical tone.

primarily constructed from 316 surgical steel and consumer-grade electronics

I can't parse this phrase. If "surgical steel and consumer-grade" is an adjectival phrase, then I don't think that many consumer grade electronics (or any electronics, really) are made of surgical steel. Also, I don't understand how to picture "316 … electronics" - should that be "316 items of consumer electronic goods"?

Otherwise, it feels like a word is missing after "surgical steel", and that there is a mix of items, some of which are surgical steel [somethings], and the rest are consumer-grade electronics.

Entity stands at

I think this needs an article: "The entity stands…". The same is true for "Forelimbs possess…" and "Exterior covering…" later in the paragraph - these sound ilke observational notes, rather than a later-written description.

a hunched posture. It resembles a distorted human in shape …

Again, my view is that "hunched" and "distorted human" are not particularly precise for clinical tone - the picture I have is of a gorilla-like posture, so it may be better to use the phrase "gorilline posture and gait" and do away with "knucklewalking" as well.

Exterior covering originally consisted of lacquered sheet steel but is presently 80% damaged or missing

How does the Foundation know what the exterior cover consisted of, or even that there was one?

SCP-2878 exhibits human or above-human intelligence but distinctly non-human psychology

I find it difficult to imagine that the Foundation can ascribe psychology to something that they've only just started to communicate with. I think this would be better describing behaviour, and letting readers judge psychology for themselves.

It possesses great proficiency in the areas of generalized logical reasoning and problem-solving

How does the Foundation know this? What logic problems have they set for this skip to solve?

It appears to lack any form of social intelligence or awareness and

How does Addendum 1 display social intelligence of the skip?

SCP-2878 is predatory and extremely hostile to humans, attacking on sight

Attacking on sight is not predatory behaviour. Predators hunt, which is quite different. How does the Foundation know that this skip is predatory?

Victims are pinned down and vivisected, having a number of their organs removed and prepared for transplantation

Addendum 1 says the relevant incident was the first time that the skip had attempted to integrate new parts. As a result, I don't think the Foundation can be so certain that this is what the skip always does to its victims.

All senses appear to function normally

How can the Foundation know that taste and smell are functioning normally? Can taste function normally - I presume the tongue is missing along with the lower jaw.

vocal chords and central nervous system

Vocal "cords" is correct - although "vocal folds" is even more accurate.

And if this thing has vocal cords, but no mouth, tongue or lips, how could it possibly create sounds that are intelligible as words. Try it - hold your mouth open, don't move your lips or tongue at all, and say "help". Or "amigo". Or a series of numbers in Spanish. There's no way that the Foundation would have understood what the skip is saying.

Hello, SCP-2878

How is this thing expected to know it's name?

[SCP-2878 does not display any physical signs of having noticed the drone.]

How does the Foundation know that its hearing is working, as suggested above?

That said, one thing I think is helping this is the complete lack of explanation of why this exists or where it came from. If anyone suggests adding it, I encourage you to refuse - this works best as an unexplained monstrosity that the Foundation happens to have around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After giving it due consideration, I've decided to take out the offending part of Addendum 2 and replace with something a bit less combative.

I did this mostly because I agree that it's just a riff on the whole "keter duty" thing which is really tired, and I've certainly downvoted SCPs for it in the past. But it also just seems prudent, since even if you like that bit taking it out won't ruin the article, but for a lot of people who don't like it, it's a total dealbreaker.

Let me know what you think of the new bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same here.

Overall nice to read, but a "we may take it into consideration" would sound better in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not completely sold on the final note, but oh my God do I love me some body horror. I don't think I've ever run into something quite like this — machines wanting/trying to become human, sure, but not in this particular way. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconded; what it adds to me is the human element colliding with the dispassionate detachment the Foundation requires. They could destroy this thing easily- it's just metal and flesh. They aren't protecting the world from this thing. They're protecting this thing from every sane person's first though: kill the monster.

The bigger thing is that he was apparently sufficiently okay with the body-part stealing monstrosity prior to the incident… but when his colleague dies, now its not just a bunch of body parts. Its part of a person he had conversations with, someone with a name. That memo is the evidence of Dr. G loosing his faith in the Foundation's basic philosophy; the idea that they can both allow something like this to live and still have a regard for human life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me, it was more that Dr. G misstepped by attempting to upgrade it to Keter (when it quite clearly isn't). Being horrified is one thing, which we'd expect, but being horrified to the point of mistake-making isn't always a guaranteed narrative direction, I've found. Usually, we get folks asking for some time off or a transfer.

I'm not saying it never happens. There are definitely other examples. I simply find it a "show, don't tell" way of demonstrating the ways Dr. G breaks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What do we really get in terms of character development, though? That Dr. G is horrified by a body-part stealing monstrosity and would rather it not kill more people? That describes pretty much anyone who isn't a psychopath. And I would hope that we're past the point where we need to establish that the Foundation doesn't necessarily need to be made up entirely of people who have complete disregard for human life.

I actually agree that it's got a Series I feel to it, in a good way. But tacking that note on the end introduces some very Series III problems into the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And I didn't get that feeling at all. Yes, we all know that it's disturbing, but the note shows character development for Dr. G, and his reaction. One of the main things I like to see is that although the researchers and containment specialists are competent and professional, they are still very much human and fallible, ending up on relying on each other not to make mistakes. The end note does just this.

It is a very Series I feel, the last addendum, which is left as an exercise to the reader if it's a good or a bad thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a nicely meta element

I will say that there's better ways to do metacommentary on that kind of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That ending note just wrecks this. If you've done your job well here (and I would say that up to this point you have), it should be pretty obvious that a weird metal construct sporting pieces of your former co-worker would be fucking disturbing. Having it spelled out explicitly, in the context of the endless and mostly pointless squabbles about object class no less, pulls the rug out from under me as a reader. Instead of finishing and going "hmm, yeah, that would be pretty fucked up" as I think about, I get Site Director McAuthor jabbing me in the ribs going "yeah, pretty scary, huh? Just wanted to make sure you got the point!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting. I was kind of afraid of that, but when I posted the draft people specifically pointed addendum 2 out as something they liked, so I kept it.

If more people have opinions on this either way, I'd love to hear them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed (with Deci) about the disciplinary tone of the note. There was another, earlier skip posted a few months ago that had a similar ending note to an emotionally-wounded worker being reprimanded for showing emotions towards a former colleague who had become tainted by an SCP (which was later edited out, happily) and I didn't care for that angle then, and I don't like it here. The entire line "Please refrain…" could easily be removed. However, I also don't entirely buy that the request to Keter would have been made in the first place. Considered, but actually asked for? Eh….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree. It's a nicely meta element, showing that the in-universe researchers sometimes make the same mistake the out-of-universe authors make - conflating danger from the SCP object with object class, rather than difficulty of containment.

Regardless, I like it. As I said in the review, this reminds me (in a good way) of the clockwork robots from Doctor Who S28E4 "The Girl in the Fireplace", trying to repair a mechanical construct with biological parts. It takes it a step further though, as this is a robot(?) that's trying to become biological (hence "Reverse Cyborg").

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was fine with it up until the final note.

but object classifications are to be based on objective threat assessment, not your personal level of discomfort. Please refrain from making such frivolous requests in the future.

This kind of thing has been done and tired and seems like a weak end to a strong article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just really brutal, but I really enjoy that about it. I find it pretty well done all around. Good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well-written, contains the story element where you're learning more about the object and the word around it as the article progresses. You get characterization of Drs. G and Bhatia, and the site director, as well as enough conjecture about its motivations to come up with a couple different possibilities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Good twist on the classic sci-fi concept and it reminds me of an even more violent Mal, which is pretty surprising.
